name: Mint Julep
story: The drink of the south. The french word julep is derived from the Arab word julab which was a drink made with rose petals. Mint replaced the rose and mixed with American whiskey or rum, the Mint Julep as it is now called became very popular in both Europe and North America. Mint Julep's became the Kentucky Derby's official drink in 1938.
method: With clean hands (of course), squeeze around 14-16 mint leaves in your hand to release fragrance and coat the entire rim and inside of julep mug with bruised bouquet. Leave in mug and add other ingredients, lightly muddle to break up the sugar cube. Fill 2/3's full of crushed ice and swizzle. Top up with crushed ice and garnish with mint sprigs (and drizzle of Jamaican rum).
ingredientsText: bourbon, simple, white sugar cube, mint

name: Mother-In-Law
story: From Ted “Dr Cocktail” Haigh: The Mother-In-Law was truly obscure, coming to him from Brooks Baldwin by way of Chuck Taggart. As told to Haigh by Brooks, from Vintage Spirits and Forgotten Cocktails: My grandmother (born in New Orleans in 1895) inherited this recipe from her mother-in-law shortly before the beginning of the First World War. As specified in the original recipe, my mother concocted this libation by the quart and stored it in an antique lead crystal decanter. Informed that science had linked lead crystal to lead poisoning, my grandmother said: “a pretty bottle, so hush”
method: Add all ingredients to chilled Boston glass, add cracked ice and stir for approximately 25-30 seconds, strain into chilled glass. Garnish with a cherry.

name: Sidecar
story: As per most classic cocktails, the exact origin of this drink remain slightly unclear. The first recipes for the Sidecar appear in 1922, in Harry MacElhone's "Harry's ABC of Mixing Cocktails" and Robert Vermeire's "Cocktails and How to Mix Them". In early editions of MacElhone's book, he cites the inventor as Pat MacGarry, "the Popular bar-tender at Buck's Club, London," but in later editions he cites himself. Vermiere states, "This cocktail is very popular in France. It was first introduced in London by MacGarry, the celebrated bar-tender of Buck's Club."

It was a combination of the recently available rich, dark Italian Vermouth bartender's had gotten their hands on, American Whiskey and bitters. It first appeared in print in a recipe book in Harry Johnson's Bartender's Manual in 1882 where he also lists the Brooklyn Cocktail.

It was only natural that bartenders and enthusiasts alike would start using this product as a substitute for sweet or Italian Vermouth in their cocktails. This cocktail is the dry vermouth version of it's predecessor the Martinez, or, to put it more simply, a gin Manhattan.

Soon people were asking for their Americano's "The Negroni way". It first appeared in "Cocktails de Paris" as a Camparinete in 1929 and then published under it's current name in the 1939 version of the "Floridita Bar Book". "Take my dignity, just don't take my Negroni..." -Sam Ross to his cruel brother Toby

name: Old Fashioned
story: A stimulating liquor composed of spirits of any kind, sugar, water and bitters. This sentence, printed in 1806, is the first know report of the word "cocktail". The Old Fashioned is essentially this, bourbon being the preferred spirit. There are also reports claiming that the Old Fashioned was a product of the Pendennis Club, Kentucky, created in the early 1880's. The oldest recipe for this drink appears in George Kappeler's Modern American Drinks, published in 1895.

name: Sazerac
story: A New Orleans icon, originally made by Apothecarian Antoine Amedee Peychauds at his shop in 1830, it was an interesting mixture of brandy and his own bitters, designed to soothe one's ailments. With the demand for his drink high, the drink started popping up in bars all over New Orleans, in particular the Merchants Exchange Coffee House. The owner, also a liquor importer, started making the drink with a type of Cognac called Sazerac-du-Forge et Fils. The bar changed hands and the new owner renamed it the Sazerac Coffee House. Rye whiskey replaced the Cognac somewhere around the 1870's when Cognac became very difficult to obtain.
method: Fill whiskey glass with crushed ice and 1/4oz absinthe. Allow to chill while you prepare the Sazerac. For Sazerac, add sugar cube to chilled Boston glass, douse in Peychauds Bitters, add a barspoon of club soda and muddle. Add spirits, fill with cracked ice and stir roughly 25 times.
